title: Why Writing Your Book Alone Is the Biggest Mistake You'll Make
guestName: Dr. Shana Hartman and Dr. Cindy Urbanski
guestBio: Dr. Shana Hartman and Dr. Cindy Urbanski are the co-founders of Synergy Publishing Group and creators of the Rebel Writers program. They serve as 'book midwives,' helping mission-driven women transform their stories into world-changing books through an embodied, community-centered approach to writing.
excerpt: What if the secret to finally writing your book isn't discipline or perfect timing, but community and permission to be messy? The book midwives who helped birth The Manage Her book reveal why women write differently—and how to honor your unique process.
metaDescription: Discover why writing your book alone is a mistake. Learn the embodied approach to authorship that's transforming how women write and publish their stories.
keyTakeaways: Writing is an embodied act that requires community, nourishment, and support—not isolation.
The transformational question for any manuscript is: 'What absolutely has to be in this book?'
Women write differently than men and need processes that honor their unique creative rhythms.
Your body knows your story before your mind does—trust your instincts about what wants to be shared.
There's never a perfect time to write a book, just like having a baby or starting a business.
Legacy authors don't write just one book—they embrace writing as part of their identity.
The people you least expect will be most transformed by your words and story.

pullQuotes.text: Your body knows your story before your mind does
What absolutely has to be in this book?
Writing doesn't happen in a vacuum—you need community, feedback, and care

timestamps.label: Why Writing is an Embodied Act

content:
Here's what no one tells you about writing a book: the hardest part isn't finding the words. It's giving yourself permission to believe your story matters. And then, it's refusing to do it alone.

In this transformative episode, Aimee sits down with the women who changed everything about how she approached authorship—Dr. Shana Hartman and Dr. Cindy Urbanski, the founders of Synergy Publishing Group and co-creators of the Rebel Writers program. These aren't just book coaches. They're what Aimee calls book midwives—women who understand that birthing a book is as intimate and powerful as any other creative act of bringing life into the world.

Writing Is an Embodied Act

Forget everything you think you know about the solitary writer, hunched over a laptop in a coffee shop. Shana and Cindy have discovered something revolutionary: your body knows your story before your mind does. Writing isn't just an intellectual exercise—it's a full-body experience that requires nourishment, movement, and community.

At their retreat property, Synergy House in North Carolina, something magical happens when women spread their manuscript pages across every surface, practice yoga between writing sessions, and eat real food prepared by someone who cares about their creative process. This isn't indulgence—it's recognition that creativity requires care.

You've been conditioned to believe that writing requires discipline, isolation, and grinding through the hard parts alone. But what if that approach is exactly what's been keeping you stuck? What if the reason you haven't finished your book isn't because you lack talent or time, but because you're trying to birth something significant without the support system that makes creation possible?

The Question That Changes Everything

When Aimee was struggling with her manuscript, overwhelmed by everything she wanted to include, Shana asked her the question that transformed the entire process: "What absolutely has to be in this book?" Not what could be included. Not what might be interesting. What absolutely, without question, had to be there.

This isn't about cutting corners or settling for less. It's about clarity. It's about understanding that not every brilliant insight you've ever had belongs in this particular book. It's about killing your darlings—those beautiful passages that you love but that don't serve the core message your readers need to hear.

This question will save you months of spinning your wheels. When you're clear on what absolutely has to be in your book, every other decision becomes easier. Does this story serve that core purpose? Does this chapter move your reader closer to the transformation you're promising? If not, it might be brilliant—but it doesn't belong here.

How Women Write Differently

Here's something the traditional publishing world doesn't want to acknowledge: women write differently than men. Not better or worse—differently. And most writing advice is built for a masculine creative process that doesn't honor how you actually work best.

Maybe you need to talk through your ideas before you write them down. Maybe you need to record voice notes while you're driving and have someone transcribe them later. Maybe you need to write in community, with other women who understand that your creative process might be messy, non-linear, and deeply intuitive.

Cindy brings thirty years of teaching writing experience and a PhD in K-12 Urban Literacy to this work. She's witnessed the power shift that happens when writers are encouraged to tell their stories in their own words, using their own process. You don't need to force yourself into someone else's writing system. You need to find the approach that honors how you actually create.

The Myth of Perfect Timing

You're waiting for the perfect time to write your book. When the kids are older. When work calms down. When you feel more confident. When you have more time. Here's the truth that Shana and Cindy want you to understand: there's never a perfect time to write a book, just like there's never a perfect time to have a baby or get a puppy.

Life doesn't pause for your creative dreams. But life also doesn't require you to choose between showing up for what matters to you and showing up for everyone else. The women who write books aren't the ones with perfect circumstances—they're the ones who decide their voice matters enough to figure out how to make it work with imperfect circumstances.

Your book doesn't need you to have unlimited time or perfect focus. Your book needs you to start where you are, with what you have, surrounded by people who believe in what you're creating.

Legacy Authorship

This isn't just about writing one book. Shana and Cindy work with legacy authors—women who understand that stepping into authorship is stepping into an identity, not just completing a project. When you write your first book, you're not just sharing one story. You're claiming your voice. You're declaring that what you have to say matters. You're joining a lineage of women who refused to stay silent.

Legacy authorship means building a creative partnership that spans multiple books. It means having midwives who understand your voice, your process, and your vision. It means never having to birth a book alone again.

The people you least expect will be transformed by your words. Your story—the one you think is too ordinary, too messy, too specific to your experience—will be exactly what someone else needs to hear. But only if you're brave enough to let it be messy, to do it imperfectly, and to refuse to do it alone.

Your book is waiting. Not for perfect circumstances, but for you to believe it matters enough to begin.
